graphdb_rdf.metric_question: How do the transparency exceptions for open-source general-purpose AI models interact with its copyright obligations, and what happens if an open-source model is later found to present systemic risk?
graphdb_rdf.metric_answer: The interaction between transparency exceptions for open-source general-purpose AI models and copyright obligations under the EU AI Act is nuanced. 1. **Transparency Exceptions**: The EU AI Act provides specific exceptions for free and open-source AI components. These exceptions are designed to facilitate compliance with transparency requirements while recognizing the unique nature of open-source models. The relevant entity is [exceptions provided to free and open-source AI components](https://euaiact.eu/resource/Policy/exceptions_provided_to_free_and_open-source_AI_components), which outlines how these models can be treated differently compared to proprietary systems. 2. **Copyright Obligations**: Providers of general-purpose AI models, including open-source models, have specific obligations under the EU AI Act. These obligations include ensuring that the models do not present undue risks and comply with applicable legal standards. The obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models are detailed in the entity [obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models](https://euaiact.eu/resource/Obligation/obligations_on_providers_of_general-purpose_AI_models). 3. **Systemic Risk**: If an open-source model is later found to present systemic risk, it may trigger additional obligations for the provider. The entity [general-purpose AI model with systemic risk](https://euaiact.eu/resource/AIModel/general-purpose_AI_model_with_systemic_risk) indicates that models identified with systemic risks must adhere to stricter compliance measures. This includes the potential for revisiting transparency requirements and ensuring that the model aligns with the regulatory framework to mitigate identified risks. In summary, while transparency exceptions allow for some flexibility in open-source models, they must still navigate copyright obligations and potential risks associated with their deployment. If systemic risks are identified post-release, additional compliance measures will be necessary to align with the EU AI Act's requirements.
